(Romanian pronunciation: [[poliˈtehnika timiˈʃo̯ara]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Romanian "Help:IPA/Romanian")), commonly known as **Politehnica Timișoara** or simply **Poli Timișoara**, was a Romanian [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club from [Timișoara](/wiki/Timi%C8%99oara "Timișoara"), [Timiș County](/wiki/Timi%C8%99_County "Timiș County"), established in 1921[[1]](#cite_note-founding-1) and dissolved in 2012. During its existence, the team won two [Cupa României](/wiki/Cupa_Rom%C3%A2niei "Cupa României") trophies and was twice runner-up in the [Liga I](/wiki/Liga_I "Liga I"). It amassed 49 seasons in the latter competition.

in the first season.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Shortly thereafter (from 1950[[8]](#cite_note-8)), the club was renamed Știința Timișoara, in line with the desired nomenclature of the times. In spite of suffering its first relegation in 1951,[[9]](#cite_note-9) the decade was an unusually consistent one, with the club returning swiftly to the top division[[10]](#cite_note-10) and staying there until the season 1959.[[11]](#cite_note-11) The high-point of the Ştiinţa years was winning the [1957–58 Romanian Cup](/wiki/1957%E2%80%9358_Cupa_Rom%C3%A2niei "1957–58 Cupa României"), a 1–0 victory against [Progresul București](/wiki/AS_Progresul_Bucure%C8%99ti "AS Progresul București"),[[12]](#cite_note-History-12) with the club finishing joint first in the league in the same season, but losing out on goal difference.[[2]](#cite_note-GSP-2)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

This article is about the original football club named Politehnica Timișoara. For the side which inherited the fanbase and is allowed to use the FC Politehnica brand and records, see [SSU Politehnica Timișoara](/wiki/SSU_Politehnica_Timi%C8%99oara "SSU Politehnica Timișoara"). For other uses, see [Politehnica Timișoara](/wiki/Politehnica_Timi%C8%99oara_(disambiguation) "Politehnica Timișoara (disambiguation)").

**Fotbal Club Politehnica Timișoara** (Romanian pronunciation: [[poliˈtehnika timiˈʃo̯ara]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Romanian "Help:IPA/Romanian")), commonly known as **Politehnica Timișoara** or simply **Poli Timișoara**, was a Romanian [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club from [Timișoara](/wiki/Timi%C8%99oara "Timișoara"), [Timiș County](/wiki/Timi%C8%99_County "Timiș County"), established in 1921[[1]](#cite_note-founding-1) and dissolved in 2012. During its existence, the team won two [Cupa României](/wiki/Cupa_Rom%C3%A2niei "Cupa României") trophies and was twice runner-up in the [Liga I](/wiki/Liga_I "Liga I"). It amassed 49 seasons in the latter competition.

Named after and initially run by the [Polytechnic University of Timișoara](/wiki/Polytechnic_University_of_Timi%C8%99oara "Polytechnic University of Timișoara"), the club was founded in 1921 by mathematician [Traian Lalescu](/wiki/Traian_Lalescu "Traian Lalescu"). After two decades spent in the regional leagues, Politehnica promoted to the top flight in 1948. It claimed its first trophy in the [1957–58 season](/wiki/1957%E2%80%9358_Cupa_Rom%C3%A2niei "1957–58 Cupa României"), when it defeated [Progresul București](/wiki/FC_Progresul_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Progresul București") in the national cup final. Internationally, "the White-Violets" made their [European](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA") debut in the [1978–79 UEFA Cup](/wiki/1978%E2%80%9379_UEFA_Cup "1978–79 UEFA Cup"), when they went past [MTK Hungária](/wiki/MTK_Budapest_FC "MTK Budapest FC") in the first round.

Politehnica obtained its highest result in the national championship after finishing second in the [2008–09 Liga I](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_Liga_I "2008–09 Liga I"). It repeated the performance in the [2010–11 season](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_Liga_I "2010–11 Liga I"), but was relegated after not being able to obtain a new participation license due to unpaid debts, and was eventually dissolved in 2012.

Following Politehnica Timișoara's dissolution, two entities claimed to be its moral successors—[ACS Poli Timișoara](/wiki/ACS_Poli_Timi%C8%99oara "ACS Poli Timișoara") and [SSU Politehnica Timișoara](/wiki/SSU_Politehnica_Timi%C8%99oara "SSU Politehnica Timișoara"). The Polytechnic University, the owner of the brand and all of FC Politehnica records, ceded these for free use to ACS Poli between 2012 and 2021, but then moved them to SSU Politehnica starting from 2021. SSU also inherited the old club's fanbase since inception.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Politehnica_Timi%C8%99oara&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Beginnings and lower leagues (1921–1945)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Politehnica_Timi%C8%99oara&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Beginnings and lower leagues (1921–1945)")]

The club was founded in 1921 by the [Polytechnic University of Timișoara](/wiki/Polytechnic_University_of_Timi%C8%99oara "Polytechnic University of Timișoara") under the name **Societatea Sportivă Politehnica**.[[1]](#cite_note-founding-1)[[2]](#cite_note-GSP-2)

Its initial aim was to provide an opportunity for university students to work on their fitness within a competitive environment. The logistics of the sport proved problematic, as there were limited financial means available. Thanks to contributions gathered from university professors and employees, the club bought their first football kits, with white-black vertical stripes, and rented the "Patria" football stadium. It was not until 1928 that the club developed its own training grounds, "Politehnica", which were built by volunteers. Players for the team were chosen on merit from the pool of Timișoara students and high-schoolers, who trained after school hours during the week and played football on weekends. The football landscape in the city was already developed at that time, with [CAT](/wiki/CA_Timi%C8%99oara "CA Timișoara"), [RGMT](/wiki/CAM_Timi%C8%99oara "CAM Timișoara") and [Chinezul Timișoara](/wiki/Chinezul_Timi%C8%99oara "Chinezul Timișoara") dominating locally.[[2]](#cite_note-GSP-2)

After spending three years in the District Championships II, Politehnica won promotion to the first tier in 1924, by defeating Kadima Timișoara. In 1926, Austrian coach of Italian origin, [Tony Cargnelli](/wiki/Tony_Cargnelli "Tony Cargnelli") came to train CAT, but he also worked as a volunteer for Politehnica, thus becoming the first coach of the club.[[3]](#cite_note-cargnelli-3) He managed both clubs including when they played against each other, after Politehnica's 3–0 win over CA from 1927 in the District Championships I, the *Universul* newspaper wrote: "The people of [Timișoara](/wiki/Timi%C8%99oara "Timișoara") had the opportunity to see a beautiful game between two teams that each in their own way showed what they owed to the joint coach Cargnelli".[[3]](#cite_note-cargnelli-3) Cargnelli finished the 1926–27 District Championships I with CA on the fifth place and with Politehnica on the second with 23 points, just one below first place Chinezul Timișoara who qualified to the [national championship](/wiki/1927%E2%80%9328_Divizia_A "1927–28 Divizia A").[[3]](#cite_note-cargnelli-3) The club became established in the years to come, even finishing 2nd in the 1926–27 District Championships I, when Politehnica lost out to Chinezul by a single point, who were one of Romania's most famous football names at the time. However, the competitive level could not be easily sustained by a university club, as it was subject to the inflow and outflow of players conditioned by their student status. After a decline towards the end of the decade, the low-point came at the beginning of the 1930s, between 1931 and 1933, when due to insufficient material resources, Politehnica had to suspend its football activities.[[2]](#cite_note-GSP-2) It reappeared in 1934 but remained a modest club, with mid-table classifications in the District Championships I, as well as the [Divizia C](/wiki/Divizia_C "Divizia C") and [Divizia B](/wiki/Divizia_B "Divizia B"), once they were founded. As war beckoned, the national championships were suspended and all football activities reduced to friendly matches and the "Cupa Eroilor" (1943–44).

Until the second World War, Politehnica was far from the number one Timișoara football club. Chinezul and then [Ripensia](/wiki/FC_Ripensia_Timi%C8%99oara "FC Ripensia Timișoara") won multiple Romanian championships, whereas the students' club failed to achieve similar results.[[4]](#cite_note-4) It did, however, propel several players to the [Romania national football team](/wiki/Romania_national_football_team "Romania national football team"), with the likes of [Sfera](/wiki/Laz%C4%83r_Sfera "Lazăr Sfera"), Ignuţa, [Deheleanu](/wiki/Vasile_Deheleanu "Vasile Deheleanu"), [Chiroiu](/wiki/Vasile_Chiroiu "Vasile Chiroiu"), [Pop](/wiki/Iulian_Pop_(footballer) "Iulian Pop (footballer)"), Protopopescu and [Sepi](/wiki/Gra%C8%9Bian_Sepi "Grațian Sepi") all wearing the national jerseys.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

### Becoming one of the city's notable clubs (1945–1991)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Politehnica_Timi%C8%99oara&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Becoming one of the city's notable clubs (1945–1991)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Dan_P%C4%83ltini%C5%9Fanu.jpg/250px-Dan_P%C4%83ltini%C5%9Fanu.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dan_P%C4%83ltini%C5%9Fanu.jpg)

[Dan Păltinișanu](/wiki/Dan_P%C4%83ltini%C8%99anu "Dan Păltinișanu") totalled 271 league matches for Politehnica Timișoara between 1973 and 1983. The [largest stadium](/wiki/Stadionul_Dan_P%C4%83ltini%C8%99anu "Stadionul Dan Păltinișanu") in the city is named after him.

With Romania under a communist regime, these decades were a challenge for Politehnica, as the club represented an educational institution of the highest tier. The numerous promotions and relegations between the first two national leagues were contrasted by the two Romanian cups won and the club's first forays into international football.[[2]](#cite_note-GSP-2)

Politehnica was first promoted to Romanian top league, the [Divizia A](/wiki/Liga_I "Liga I"), in 1948,[[6]](#cite_note-6) and played under the name **CSU Timișoara** in the first season.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Shortly thereafter (from 1950[[8]](#cite_note-8)), the club was renamed Știința Timișoara, in line with the desired nomenclature of the times. In spite of suffering its first relegation in 1951,[[9]](#cite_note-9) the decade was an unusually consistent one, with the club returning swiftly to the top division[[10]](#cite_note-10) and staying there until the season 1959.[[11]](#cite_note-11) The high-point of the Ştiinţa years was winning the [1957–58 Romanian Cup](/wiki/1957%E2%80%9358_Cupa_Rom%C3%A2niei "1957–58 Cupa României"), a 1–0 victory against [Progresul București](/wiki/AS_Progresul_Bucure%C8%99ti "AS Progresul București"),[[12]](#cite_note-History-12) with the club finishing joint first in the league in the same season, but losing out on goal difference.[[2]](#cite_note-GSP-2)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/%C8%98tiin%C8%9Ba_Timi%C8%99oara_1965_3.jpg/250px-%C8%98tiin%C8%9Ba_Timi%C8%99oara_1965_3.jpg)](/wiki/File:%C8%98tiin%C8%9Ba_Timi%C8%99oara_1965_3.jpg)

Știința Timișoara squad in 1965

The next decade saw the club struggle to remain in the first league, particularly towards the end of the 60s. However, it was then that the club's modern identity started taking shape. Firstly, in 1963, the largest stadium in Timişoara was completed. It was initially named "1 Mai", honoring the [socialist workers' day](/wiki/International_Workers%27_Day "International Workers' Day"), before being renamed several times in the 90s and finally settling on [Stadionul Dan Păltinişanu](/wiki/Stadionul_Dan_P%C4%83ltini%C8%99anu "Stadionul Dan Păltinișanu").[[2]](#cite_note-GSP-2) Secondly, the club reverted to its previous name of *Politehnica Timișoara*[[13]](#cite_note-s1967-13) in 1966 and went to play during the next five decades on the then-erected stadium.[[2]](#cite_note-GSP-2)

When Politehnica returned to the first league in 1973, after struggling to win promotion for several years, it went on to celebrate one of its best streaks in the top flight. With the likes of [Emeric Dembrovschi](/wiki/Emerich_Dembrovschi "Emerich Dembrovschi") and [Dan Păltinişanu](/wiki/Dan_P%C4%83ltini%C8%99anu "Dan Păltinișanu") in the team, who both played for Romania and went on to become some of the most capped players in the club's history, and under the management of prof. Ion V. Ionescu, Politehnica lost that season's cup final. After managing a third place in the league with manager [Angelo Niculescu](/wiki/Angelo_Niculescu "Angelo Niculescu") in 1978, Politehnica took part in a continental competition for the first time. It was the [1978–79 UEFA Cup](/wiki/1978%E2%80%9379_UEFA_Cup "1978–79 UEFA Cup"), where *Poli* defeated [MTK Budapest](/wiki/MTK_Budapest "MTK Budapest") (2–0 and 1–2), before going down to [Honved Budapest](/wiki/Honved_Budapest "Honved Budapest") (2–0 and 0–4) in the second round.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

The club remained steady and managed to win its [second cup trophy](/wiki/1979%E2%80%9380_Cupa_Rom%C3%A2niei "1979–80 Cupa României") the following season, by beating Steaua București with 2–1, after extra time.[[12]](#cite_note-History-12) Politehnica thereby qualified for the [UEFA Cup Winners' Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup_Winners%27_Cup "UEFA Cup Winners' Cup"), where it managed to eliminate [Celtic Glasgow](/wiki/Celtic_Glasgow "Celtic Glasgow") (1–0 and 1–2), before being defeated by [West Ham United](/wiki/West_Ham_United "West Ham United") (1–0 and 0–4) in second leg.[[2]](#cite_note-GSP-2)[[15]](#cite_note-stats80-15) In spite of losing another Romanian Cup final in 1981, the club qualified once more for the UEFA Cup Winners' Cup, where they lost to [Leipzig](/wiki/1._FC_Lokomotive_Leipzig "1. FC Lokomotive Leipzig") 2–5 on aggregate.[[15]](#cite_note-stats80-15)

After being relegated in 1983, Politehnica yo-yo-ed between Divizia A and the [Divizia B](/wiki/Liga_II "Liga II"), with promotions in 1984,[[16]](#cite_note-16) 1987,[[17]](#cite_note-17) 1989[[18]](#cite_note-18) and relegations in 1986[[19]](#cite_note-19) and 1988.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Fans ironically called this period as the 'ABBA years'. As the Romanian Revolution, which started in Timișoara, signalled the end of an era, Politehnica managed to grab its most impressive result yet in European competitions, by eliminating [Atlético Madrid](/wiki/Atl%C3%A9tico_Madrid "Atlético Madrid") (2–0 and 0–1) in the [1990–91 UEFA Cup](/wiki/1990%E2%80%9391_UEFA_Cup "1990–91 UEFA Cup").[[21]](#cite_note-stats90-21)

### After privatisation (1991–2001)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Politehnica_Timi%C8%99oara&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: After privatisation (1991–2001)")]

By state order, all public institutions were forced to relinquish and reorganize any owned sports clubs in 1991, to effectively privatize them. As a result, alongside the newly organized football club appeared a non-profit association, **AFC Politehnica Timișoara**.[[22]](#cite_note-22) The latter, consisting of previous club players and staff, was mandated with owning and protecting the club records and intellectual property.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

The club's swan song near the top of Romanian football for the next decade was to be the 1991–92 season. Poli finished 5th and also reached the [Romanian Cup final](/wiki/1991%E2%80%9392_Cupa_Rom%C3%A2niei#Final "1991–92 Cupa României"), only to lose it on penalties against [Steaua București](/wiki/CSA_Steaua_Bucure%C8%99ti_(football) "CSA Steaua București (football)"). The consequent participation in the [1992–93 UEFA Cup](/wiki/1992%E2%80%9393_UEFA_Cup "1992–93 UEFA Cup"), saw the club draw against [Real Madrid](/wiki/Real_Madrid "Real Madrid") (1–1 in Timișoara), before being defeated in the return leg (0–4).[[21]](#cite_note-stats90-21) Politehnica lost several key players in the years after the forced privatization, which slowly lead to the team's downfall. In fewer than twenty four months from their draw against Real, the club was relegated to the [Divizia B](/wiki/Liga_II "Liga II") in 1994.[[24]](#cite_note-24) Despite a fast return to the first league in 1995,[[25]](#cite_note-25) Poli failed to consolidate their position and were soon relegated once more after the 1996–97 season.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

An inability to rebound lead to mounting financial pressures. The club was temporarily owned by a Timișoara based businessman between 1998 and 2000, before the local authorities accepted the bid of an Italian investor, Claudio Zambon, to take over Politehnica.[[2]](#cite_note-GSP-2)[[27]](#cite_note-27) Despite an initial financial outlay, Poli finished 15th and was relegated to the third league, Divizia C, where it had last played in 1938. To avoid such an outcome, Zambon and the local authorities struck a deal with a league two club, [Dacia Pitești](/wiki/Dacia_Pite%C8%99ti "Dacia Pitești"), and purchased their license to participate in the Divizia B.[[2]](#cite_note-GSP-2) After failing to earn promotion to top flight, the 2001–02 season posed an insurmountable challenge for Politehnica. Zambon's departure following disagreements with the local authorities meant the club found itself in dire financial straits.[[2]](#cite_note-GSP-2) Forced to use mostly youth players, Politehnica finished the season dead last, with one win and four draws to its name, but negative eight points in the standings, due to unpaid debts. Once again the club was bound to be relegated to the third division.[[2]](#cite_note-GSP-2)[[28]](#cite_note-28)

### Identity crisis, glory years and downfall (2002–2012)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Politehnica_Timi%C8%99oara&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Identity crisis, glory years and downfall (2002–2012)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Poli_Timisoara_0_-_0_Steaua_Bucuresti_%28meciul_vazut_din_tribuna%29_-_2_aprilie_2011_30.jpg/330px-Poli_Timisoara_0_-_0_Steaua_Bucuresti_%28meciul_vazut_din_tribuna%29_-_2_aprilie_2011_30.jpg)](/wiki/File:Poli_Timisoara_0_-_0_Steaua_Bucuresti_(meciul_vazut_din_tribuna)_-_2_aprilie_2011_30.jpg)

Poli Timișoara supporters during a first division game against [FC Steaua București](/wiki/FCSB "FCSB"), April 2011.

In 2002, AEK Bucharest were promoted to [Liga I](/wiki/Liga_I "Liga I"), Romanian football's top division, for the first time,[[29]](#cite_note-29) whereupon [Anton Doboș](/wiki/Anton_Dobo%C8%99 "Anton Doboș"), the club's owner, moved it to Timișoara.[[2]](#cite_note-GSP-2) It was renamed Politehnica AEK Timișoara after merging with CSU Politehnica, a club owned and run by the Politehnica University, and received the full support of local authorities and white-purple fans. After a rocky first season, which required a spectacular relegation play-off against [Gloria Buzău](/wiki/FC_Gloria_Buz%C4%83u "FC Gloria Buzău")[[30]](#cite_note-30) to avoid demotion, Poli AEK consolidated during the next season, finishing on a safe mid-table position. Moreover, starting with the 2004–05 season, the team changed its name to FCU Politehnica Timișoara, trying to reestablish its former identity.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

Financially difficulties looming, Politehnica changed ownership once more. Former president Anton Doboș stayed on at the club for another year in a new position, while Balkan Petroleum Ltd., owned by [Marian Iancu](/wiki/Marian_Iancu "Marian Iancu"), took full charge. Significant investments in the transfer market transformed the club overnight, as it received the nickname "EuroPoli" for its newly found ambitions to reach the top of Romanian football.[[2]](#cite_note-GSP-2)

During the takeover by Marian Iancu, a dispute regarding the proprietary rights for the club name, colors and records arose. After prolonged litigation, Politehnica was forced to change its name to FC Timișoara, following a decision by the Court of Arbitration for Sport.[[2]](#cite_note-GSP-2) It was deemed that the colors and records dating before 2002 were lost in favor of former Politehnica Timișoara owner, Claudio Zambon.[[2]](#cite_note-GSP-2) The Italian had struck a deal with AFC Politehnica, the non-profit association which owned said proprietary items, when he left Timișoara during the 2001–02 season.[[2]](#cite_note-GSP-2)[[32]](#cite_note-32)

Results on the pitch improved immediately after the takeover, but a leap to the Liga 1 podium proved elusive until 2008–09, when Politehnica finished runner-up, a feat repeated two seasons later. The club did, however, rejoin European football the season before that by qualifying for the [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup "UEFA Cup")[[33]](#cite_note-UEFA-33) – sixteen years after its last appearance against Atletico Madrid. More European appearances followed, culminating with the qualification for the 3rd preliminary round of the 2009–10 UEFA Champions League.[[34]](#cite_note-UCL-34) The team defeated the reigning UEFA Cup champions [Shakhtar Donetsk](/wiki/FC_Shakhtar_Donetsk "FC Shakhtar Donetsk"), but were eliminated from the competition during the playoff round, which still meant the club would take part in the [2009–10 UEFA Europa League](/wiki/2009%E2%80%9310_UEFA_Europa_League "2009–10 UEFA Europa League") group stage.[[2]](#cite_note-GSP-2)[[35]](#cite_note-stats_2000-35)

In November 2010, the Romanian Court of Appeal returned Politehnica's name, colors and records to FC Timișoara.[[2]](#cite_note-GSP-2)[[36]](#cite_note-36)

Despite finishing second in the [2010–11 Liga I](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_Liga_I "2010–11 Liga I"), the team was relegated to [Liga II](/wiki/Liga_II "Liga II") after the club failed to meet the requirements for obtaining the necessary licence to play in the first division.[[2]](#cite_note-GSP-2)[[37]](#cite_note-37) They played in the [2011–12 Liga II](/wiki/2011%E2%80%9312_Liga_II "2011–12 Liga II") under the name of Politehnica Timișoara, and gained promotion back to the [Liga I](/wiki/2012%E2%80%9313_Liga_I "2012–13 Liga I") but were again denied the licence and were dissolved in September 2012.[[2]](#cite_note-GSP-2) [ACS Recaș](/wiki/ACS_Reca%C8%99 "ACS Recaș") was moved to [Timișoara](/wiki/Timi%C8%99oara "Timișoara") and was renamed [ACS Poli Timișoara](/wiki/ACS_Poli_Timi%C8%99oara "ACS Poli Timișoara") but the fans decided to support the amateur team [ASU Politehnica Timișoara](/wiki/ASU_Politehnica_Timi%C8%99oara "ASU Politehnica Timișoara"), considering ACS Poli Timișoara to be a fraud and a political maneuver.[[2]](#cite_note-GSP-2)

The [Romanian Football Federation](/wiki/Romanian_Football_Federation "Romanian Football Federation") announced before the start of the [2021–22 Liga II](/wiki/2021%E2%80%9322_Liga_II "2021–22 Liga II") championship, that the Polytechnic University of Timișoara, the owner of the logo, history and all of Politehnica Timișoara's football records, approves the use of these by [SSU Politehnica Timișoara](/wiki/SSU_Politehnica_Timi%C8%99oara "SSU Politehnica Timișoara").[[2]](#cite_note-GSP-2) So from a legal point of view, from now on, this team will be considered as the official and legal successor of the old FC Politehnica Timișoara team.[[2]](#cite_note-GSP-2)

## Support

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Politehnica_Timi%C8%99oara&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Support")]

Historically, Poli has been the most prominent football club in Timișoara after 1945, playing consistently in either the first or the second tier of Romanian football. Local rivalries with [CFR Timișoara](/wiki/FC_CFR_Timi%C8%99oara "FC CFR Timișoara")[[38]](#cite_note-38) and [UM Timișoara](/wiki/CSP_UM_Timi%C8%99oara "CSP UM Timișoara")[[39]](#cite_note-39) were relevant until the early 2000s. Afterwards, the former was relegated to a semi-professional status in the lower leagues and the latter was dissolved in 2008.

Nationally, Politehnica Timișoara's most important rivalries were the ones with [Dinamo București](/wiki/FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Dinamo București") and [UTA Arad](/wiki/FC_UTA_Arad "FC UTA Arad"). The cities of Timișoara and [Arad](/wiki/Arad,_Romania "Arad, Romania") are located in proximity of each other in the western part of Romania; Both cities also claim to be the first place where football was played in the country. Poli fans, which now support ASU Politehnica Timișoara, had a friendly relationship with the supporters of [Rapid București](/wiki/FC_Rapid_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Rapid București") and [Borussia Mönchengladbach](/wiki/Borussia_M%C3%B6nchengladbach "Borussia Mönchengladbach").[[40]](#cite_note-Friendship-40)
